speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Felicia Carmelly, maiden name Steigman, tape three. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we arrived in Mogilev. And the-- getting off the barge was the same mess as getting off the train-- people screaming, people losing family members. Everything had to be done fast. The soldiers were screaming.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you on the barge?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess about an hour, or maybe two. It's a pretty big river, and the barge was not motorized. I don't exactly know how it was-- it was just like a big, big platform with ropes all around it. And there were some posts on the corners which the ropes were tied to. And that was supposed to be the railing. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we arrived in Mogilev. And-- I don't know why-- either because I was in a state of shock, or for whatever reason, I do not remember anything about Mogilev. I know that we didn't stay there too long. But I don't remember anything about it. And in our family, we did not talk about Transnistria. That was a subject which was supposed to be forgotten, even though everybody was suffering from it with nightmares, and paranoia, and all kinds of things. But it was never openly discussed. So I never retrieved that information from my parents. I know we were there a few days. And after that, we went on a march with a big convoy-- convoy is a column-- a big column of people-- in the mud. It was getting very cold, very muddy. And we marched for three days to a place called Shargorod, which was about 40 kilometers east of Mogilev, in the same territory called Transnistria. Some people wanted to remain in Mogilev, because they were closer to the border. And they were hoping that this mess is going to finish so they'll be closer to home. Other people heard that it's better more in the interior. So there were rumors for this, rumors for that, and nobody knew what the reality is. So-- so obviously, my parents were those who believed that it's better in the interior. So we joined this convoy. The march was a horrific experience. First of all, we slept in the fields and ditches in the rain, surrounded by the soldiers. Secondly, there was no food. Most importantly, and most traumatically, whoever couldn't keep up with the march remained behind and was shot by the soldiers or were simply left in the mud. And-- and peasants came and took their clothes off. And eventually they died or were eaten by wild animals. And of course, the people who couldn't walk were mostly elderly people, the grandparents, and the-- and children. As much as the parents wanted to carry them, in families where there were more than two children, they couldn't. So a lot of people perished on-- on this march. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when we got to-- to Mogilev, we were-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --we were allowed to find lodging by ourselves with some of the local Ukrainians. And we paid for that. So we went-- we lived with a-- in a hut-- very primitive hut-- no toilet, no running water, no furniture, earth floors, just a stove. There was some kind of a primitive stove in which they were baking bread and cooking at the same time. It was made-- the whole hut was made out of dry manure, and then it was painted on top of that. And-- so it was cold. It was very cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Shargorod?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shargorod. So in this hut there were-- there were five people living before, which was a very old lady who we called her babushka. And I never knew what-- nobody ever knew what her name was, because that's how she was called. And then there was her daughter-in-law, who was Bella, who was an absolutely gorgeous, absolutely gorgeous young woman-- black hair, black eyes, carried herself like a queen. And she had one son, Tosia, who looked very much like her. He was 10 years old-- my age-- gorgeous, but stupid. And-- [LAUGHS] --that was from her first marriage. And then she had another son, a five-year-old, with this second husband, who was the real grandchild of babushka. And he was a redhead, and freckled, and ugly, and smart like 10 devils, and behaved like 10 devils. I mean, he knew everything-- what's going on in-- in the huts around us, and where to steal, and what to get, and what to-- and-- and-- he was our lifeline, like with the outside world. We did not stay in the same hut with my Auntie Mila, and my Uncle Armin, and my grandmother, and-- and neither with my paternal grandparents. We stayed with some-- some other people. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And then we started running out of money. And that's when the barter system took off. Barter means you exchange a pair of shoes, for example, for a carton of potatoes, or a shirt, or a coat for a-- a little bag of flour, or for some beets, or-- or what have you. And we had it not so bad, because [NON-ENGLISH] who was Beile's husband, who was taken into the Soviet army, he used to work in some kind of a [NON-ENGLISH] which is a collective Soviet farm. And he had a lot of connections with non-Jews who used to bring to the hut food. And of course, hey, it was great, because they got a coat for a little-- for a few potato. But then eventually, we ran out. And I-- I had very long hair-- long, dark, beautiful hair, which was below my waist. And my mother used to sit and brush my hair. And-- and soon we got lice, because there was no way of washing. They were used to lice. Lice was a part of their life. With the way I was brought up and with my mother's compulsive cleanliness, lice or bed bugs-- there weren't any beds, but there were bed bugs. Seeing these things crawl on us was horrendous. It was just very, very painful-- very traumatic. And-- and-- and Tosia would sit for hours watching my mother play around with my hair and be sort of-- I-- I he was my first boyfriend, if you can call it that way. He was gorgeous. None of my boyfriends ever came to his standard in terms of beauty. But he-- we weren't even talking. We were just sitting looking at each other. Anyway, so in order to keep the lice away from my hair, my parents bartered some things for naphtha-- naphtha is some kind of a liquid fuel-- because if you washed your hair with naphtha, that kills the lice-- for a while. But it's very-- it's very rough on your scalp. It-- it burns the hell out of it. And eventually, they didn't have enough things to-- but we-- food was more important than naphtha. So my hair had to be cut, and that was a very big source of sorrow for me. Because I took great pride in my hair. And besides, I didn't know if Tosia will still like me if I don't have that gorgeous, long hair. And I wanted him to like me. I mean, that was one bright spot for me in Shargorod. So my hair was cut. And then there weren't any things to barter anymore. So the peasants used to come and bring wool, but that wool was not like we know wool today. It was very rough. And they used to bring some kind of wires for knitting the wool. And all the women were knitting sweaters, or scarves, or socks, or gloves. And the men were taken out to labor. And after knitting only for 5 or 10 minutes, because the wool was so rough, and these wires were so rough, everybody's hands were bleeding. And the skin was coming off. And those sweaters, they-- they all were red or reddish. And that that was not their color. They were red from blood. But it was one way of getting a bag of potatoes, or whatever. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For somehow, babushka managed to get vodka from somebody who brought it. And she had-- there was one piece of furniture in the house. It was a broken stool, and then there was this big sort of shelf structure. And she put the vodka on the very top shelf. I-- I cannot to this day understand how she got to it. But she did. But one day, Tosia went after-- no, no, the little one, Musia went after this bottle. And-- and there was a big scandal, because he-- he had been stealing from the vodka. And he put it back. Like he took a few swigs and put it back. But he was caught one day by her, and she nearly killed him. She was swearing for days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knitted. The men worked outside. My father had the misfortune of working at the mass graves. There were hundreds and hundreds of deaths every day from starvation, from typhus, from dysentery, from freezing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people were shot. Some people were beaten to death. But most people died from the natural causes of typhus, lice, dysentery, hunger, and-- and frost. And a lot of people died on the roads, of course, because they were taking people from one place, moving them to another. We were lucky we stayed in this same place for 3 and 1/2 years. I don't know how we were so lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe my Uncle Armin was in cahoots with the administration. He was stealing from them, and he was getting whatever he was stealing-- ammunition, and salt, and is it called back fat? That white from pork-- that fat-- meat from pork-- he managed to steal that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was groups of partisans in the forest, and he somehow managed to find a way to transfer that to-- to them. And that was very important. So he had all kinds of connections. And-- and maybe that's why we stayed put for 3 and 1/2 years in the same place. There were many days in which we couldn't knit fast enough, or we didn't get wool, or whatever, when there was absolutely no food. And-- and it was a horrible life. It was. You never knew who opens the door, and what they want, and if they're going to shoot you on the spot-- the soldiers-- or-- it was a constant threat to our lives, day and night. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Before of we crossed the Dniester, because my mother is so clean, she decided to wash me and herself of the mud and of the excrement. So very early one morning, she took me, when-- when we thought that the gendarmes are still asleep-- she took me to the river. By that time-- by that time we knew where the river was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took me to the river, and on the shores of the river I saw some incredible sights. There were limbs of animals, and limbs of people, and Hebrew prayer books all stained with blood, and torn Torahs, and-- and heads, and arms, and fingers, and breasts, and-- and it was all just lying around. And-- and we-- we found out later that they slaughtered a lot of the people from [NON-ENGLISH]. A lot of the Jewish people were slaughtered there.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we cried, and we washed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there were bodies floating by on the river. And on the way back, to the-- we stayed-- we-- we did not sleep in the field. We slept in a-- in a barn, some kind of a barn, which only had a-- it had a roof. And then it had some walls made from logs, but it also had these very big openings for animals to go in. And it had-- it had some straw. I'm going back now a little bit, because there was a night that I will never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in-- so in order to stop the wind from blowing, people hung up on the nails-- they hung blankets from our luggage. And-- and everybody was standing on guard. So the men were on guard. Like, everybody else was on the floor in the straw, and the men-- so my-- my Uncle Armin was on guard at one of these openings for two hours, and then he would go and lie down, and my father would go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at 12 o'clock at night one night, we heard this horrendous howling out in the field. And it was exactly like wolves. And I was familiar with wolves, because in the mountains there are wolves. I was familiar with the sound of wolves. And that was exactly the same. And everybody stood up from the straw. And everybody ran to the openings and pulled the blankets away to see what's going on out there. And I did too. And I was-- there was a man standing in front of me. And I sort of pushed his legs apart a little bit so I can see from between his legs what's happening outside. And I saw these-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --these creatures. To me, they looked like phantoms, because they were all dressed in white. And these white-- whatever they were wearing-- was fluttering in the wind. And they were running across the field, and the soldiers were shooting them. And they were howling. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after about 1/2 an hour or an hour, they were all lying in the field and drenched in blood. And the-- there-- there was no more noise, no more shooting, no more nothing outside. So we didn't know what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody went back. And again, it went back to the guarding the openings. And some people were lying on the floor on-- in the straw.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the morning, my father, again, bribed one of the soldiers. And the soldier told him that these were people who had been taken out from mental hospitals from [NON-ENGLISH]-- Jewish people. They were not given their clothing. They were told that they can take the sheets from the hospital beds, which were all white, and wrap them around themselves. And then they were put into one, or two, or three boxcars coupled to the end of our train, to also be deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were kept in the cold. And they were kept in the dark. And they were kept without food. And they were crazy to begin with. They were mentally disturbed. So at 12 o'clock at night, the gendarmes decided to open the doors and let them out into the field. And they behaved like animals. They were hungry, and they were running around screaming. And-- and-- and then the-- the soldier told my father, well, we used them as target practice. What else can we do for fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- I'll never forget that, because some of these people came close to the openings. Some of them actually banged, ran into the external-- to the-- to the outside of that barn from logs, and hit their heads on the-- and I could see their faces. And they looked human, but they weren't really human.

speaker: interviewee
text: Their eyes were like the eyes of animals. They were like shining. And they were-- they had a different look. And I don't know if it was because they were scared, or hungry, or all of it together. That's another recurrent dream that I have to this day. Now we're going back to Shargorod. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] One very traumatic experience for me was the fact that there were no toilets. And I was shy to begin with, and very prudish, and very-- like, when we have to go to the shvitz, I didn't like to undress in the good times. And here I had to do my thing in front of other people in the backyard. And because we were now 60 new people, plus the 4 of them-- 17 I think there were. I think there were 21 people in the hut. Everybody went in the backyard, which wasn't very big. It was the size of a postage stamp. So that filled up with shit pretty quickly-- pardon me. So-- so then there was a decision that people will not go in the backyard anymore. They will go around a pond. And-- and then this-- that will be shoveled into the pond from time to time. That was very, very terrible for me, because I had to pull my pants down in public. I was 11, 12, you know. And I had to go in public. And it was-- and everybody was sitting around. It was-- it like became a part of everyday life. But I could not. That was left a-- to this day, I cannot use-- I do not use a public toilet. If I can possibly avoid it, I don't. Even though it's private, and closed, and clean, if I could possibly avoid it, I don't. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I said that my father was working at the mass graves. And that was-- that what-- that killed his spirit, because there were hundreds of people who died every day. Not all of them could be buried. They were buried in mass graves. A lot of them died in the winter, and the-- then the earth was too frozen to dig the mass graves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were kept beside the synagogue wall there-- heaps and mountains of people that were frozen. And they-- they froze to one another. So it became one big mountain of frozen dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people would go and look at their children, or their grandparents, or their aunts, or their uncle. And then, when spring came, it was a mad rush to bury these people before they decay. Because that is another way of spreading epidemics, and because it would have been also much too painful-- more painful for people to see them decay. And the only way to bury these people was to hack them to pieces from the frozen mountain-- to hack lumps of that frozen mountain of dead and bury them. And my father just became broken. And my father had pitch black hair and blue eyes. And in a few days, his hair changed to white. I've never seen anything like this in my life. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My mother couldn't handle the-- the dirt and the whole environment that we lived in. And her way out of this was she became paralyzed. So it was almost like psychologically, she couldn't do anything about it, so she had immobilized her body too. I can't do anything about it anyways, so here I am. I'm a blob of nothing. And for a year, she-- she didn't move. The lower-- the lower part of her body was paralyzed. In order for us to all sleep in that hut, the men had built from wooden planks low-- like low structures. And the reason for this was so that some people can sleep underneath, and some people can sleep on top, because there wasn't enough room for everybody to sleep on-- on the floor-- on the dirt floor. So some people-- the more privileged ones, or the sick ones, or the old ones slept on-- on this wooden thing, which we called a [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Then there was a time in which the soldier came to the house, a Romanian soldier. And he brought some money to my father-- money that was sent by these friends that my father had sent the money to, to Romania. That-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --I think my father bought himself out for a week or so from the job-- from this terrible job when he-- when we got this money. And the-- the Jewish committee either-- or the Jewish committee actually had to organize these people for labor, even though we were surrounded by Romanians and Ukrainians who were ferocious. It was the Jewish committee who had-- who had the task from them to organize whatever labor had to be done. And they took the money too, because they had organized an orphanage. There were a lot of kids that were roaming on the streets. Their parents had died. They had organized an orphanage, and they used this money to buy food or wood for-- for the orphanage. So those people who received money on a regular basis actually had it a little bit better, because they didn't have to go to labor all the time and because they could exchange the money for-- for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] What did you do on a daily basis? Describe a typical day for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as I got up, if it was in the summer, we would go out in the fields and work in the fields. And if it was in the winter, we would not.

speaker: interviewer
text: All day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, all day. After a while-- I believe it was after the first year-- my parents found this lady from another town, from Bukovina, who used to be a teacher. And there were five girls who used to go to her hut. And she used to talk to us for two hours, two, three times a week. I mean, we had no paper, no pencils, no books, but she used to tell us about history, about geography, about the world, about what have you. And that was very important. That was very important for the-- for our parents that-- that-- that the mind keeps going, even a little bit, you know, that you don't completely become a moron there.

speaker: interviewer
text: During his time in Shargorod, did you receive any information what was going on in the outside world?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My Uncle Armin had some information, because he was always involved with the Romanians, and the Ukrainians, and the partisans, and this and that. But he didn't tell us for a number of reasons. Number one, because the-- it was not-- not good news. Until the war turned, the news were very bad. And also because he was afraid that somebody's going to find out that he knows more than he's supposed to know. But during the last year, he asked me if I would do something for him. And I said yes. I loved him very much. And I-- my Aunt Mila was-- was also-- I loved her very much. And he said he can't tell now, but he'll tell me in a few days. But he just wants me to promise that I'll do it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a few days later, he actually got my mom and dad around too there. And he explained that he-- there was a baby in our hut. This couple from Dorohoi had the baby. And what he wanted me to do is take this baby from our hut in a little carriage that he has built himself from wood. It looked like a box. And somewhere he stole four wheels. And he put them on, and it had a handle, like.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I should take that baby and walk with it to the end of the road where our hut was. And there, I should give it to a man with a cap. I believe it was a brown cap.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- I said, sure. It was something-- it gave me something to do. It was great.

speaker: interviewer
text: So obviously, the-- the parents of this baby were involved in this too in retrospect, right? And I only found out after the war that this carriage had a double bottom. So in them-- so there was a box there, which he had loaded up with ammunition, or explosives, or food, or salt. Salt was a very important commodity at that time for people who lived in the forest. And-- and I was giving it to this guy who was somehow transferring it to the partisans in the forest. Take a break now.
